EcoTrack Mpumalanga — Provincial CCIS

A web-based platform enabling provincial government officers, municipal managers and NGO coordinators to log, track, report and align climate projects with the Paris Agreement.

🌿 Environmental Conservation🗺️ Mpumalanga📊 NQF Level 7⚡ Paris Agreement

Presentation 2 — Problem Analysis & Background

Data & Process
Modelling

4 May 2026
APB J Black 10
08:00 – 12:30
10–15 minute presentation
01 — Problem Analysis
Background & Problem Statement
Province
Mpumalanga, north-eastern South Africa. Ehlanzeni, Gert Sibande and Nkangala districts.
Coal Output
~83% of South Africa's total coal production. 12 coal-fired power stations on the Highveld.
Arable Land
~46% of South Africa's high-potential arable land. 80 000 agricultural jobs at risk.
Framework
Paris Agreement, South Africa's NDCs, Climate Change Act 2024, POPIA, DFFE alignment.
Client Interview
Just Transition Coordinator interviewed 6 March 2026.

Climate change represents one of the most pressing developmental challenges facing South Africa today. Mpumalanga sits at the intersection of this crisis — home to a significant portion of the country's coal-fired power infrastructure while simultaneously bearing the environmental consequences of decades of industrial activity.

Various stakeholders including provincial and municipal government departments, NGOs, and community-based initiatives have undertaken a growing number of climate projects across the province. Despite this, coordination remains largely fragmented — departments rely on disconnected spreadsheets, email threads, and siloed systems.

The lack of a centralised, integrated platform creates significant gaps in transparency, accountability, and evidence-based decision-making. Without a unified system, officers, managers, and coordinators cannot efficiently track project progress, monitor impact, manage funding, or produce accurate reports aligned with South Africa's national obligations under the Paris Agreement.

EcoTrack Mpumalanga proposes a web-based Information System as a centralised platform for recording, tracking, and reporting on climate change projects — built around seven core functional modules.

02 — Project Scope
In Scope vs Out of Scope
In Scope
✓
Capture and management of climate project data across all five environmental sectors
✓
Tracking of environmental impact including emissions reduction and climate vulnerability outcomes
✓
Management of climate funding and linking of funding sources to specific projects
✓
Generation of automated reports for provincial government, national departments, and funders
✓
Cross-sector dashboards for monitoring project performance and NDC alignment
✓
Role-based access control for all user types
Out of Scope
✗
Real-time sensor integration for automated environmental data collection
✗
Direct control or management of physical environmental systems
✗
Mobile-native applications for Android or iOS
✗
Advanced analytics including AI-based predictions or machine learning models
03 — Business Analyst Deliverables
Functional Requirements
Group 1 — Project RegistryFR-01 to FR-05
FR-01
The system shall allow authenticated users to create a new climate project record by providing a project name, sector, type, location, start date, and current status.
FR-02
The system shall categorise each project under at least one sector: Water, Waste, Biodiversity, Human Settlements, or Agriculture.
FR-03
The system shall flag each project as "Mitigation" or "Adaptation" with supporting notes provided by the submitting user.
FR-04
The system shall allow users to update the status of an existing project record to Planned, Active, or Completed.
FR-05
The system shall allow users to search the project registry by sector, municipality, project type, status, and date range.
Group 2 — Emissions & Vulnerability TrackingFR-06 to FR-09
FR-06
The system shall allow users to log greenhouse gas reduction data against a project, including estimated tonnes of CO₂ equivalent reduced.
FR-07
The system shall flag each project as contributing to greenhouse gas reduction, climate vulnerability reduction, or both.
FR-08
The system shall allow users to record the climate vulnerability indicator addressed by a project, selecting from flood risk, drought risk, food security, and water scarcity.
FR-09
The system shall display a cumulative emissions reduction total across all active mitigation projects on the dashboard.
Group 3 — Cross-Sector DashboardFR-10 to FR-13
FR-10
The system shall display a cross-sector dashboard showing the count and status of all active projects, filterable by sector, municipality, and date range.
FR-11
The system shall display visual summaries of project distribution across the five environmental sectors using charts or graphs.
FR-12
The system shall highlight projects that are overdue or have not been updated within the last 30 days on the dashboard.
FR-13
The system shall allow provincial administrators to view a province-wide summary and municipal managers to view a municipality-scoped summary on the same dashboard.
Group 4 — NDC & Paris Agreement AlignmentFR-14 to FR-17
FR-14
The system shall allow users to map each project to one or more of South Africa's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) targets.
FR-15
The system shall display the percentage of active projects aligned to each NDC target area on the dashboard.
FR-16
The system shall flag projects that are not yet mapped to any NDC target, prompting the responsible user to complete the alignment.
FR-17
The system shall allow policy analysts to generate a report showing all projects aligned to Paris Agreement commitments, filterable by sector and municipality.
Group 5 — Climate Finance ModuleFR-18 to FR-21
FR-18
The system shall allow users to record available climate funding windows, including the funding source, total amount available, and application deadline.
FR-19
The system shall allow users to link a project to one or more funding sources and record the amount allocated per source.
FR-20
The system shall track actual expenditure against allocated funding for each project and display the remaining balance.
FR-21
The system shall generate a funding summary report showing total funds allocated, spent, and remaining across all projects for a selected period.
Group 6 — Automated ReportingFR-22 to FR-25
FR-22
The system shall automatically generate a monthly progress report summarising new projects registered, status changes, and funding updates.
FR-23
The system shall automatically generate a quarterly environmental report including emissions data, NDC alignment status, and funding expenditure for national departments.
FR-24
The system shall allow users to export all reports in PDF format.
FR-25
The system shall notify designated users via the platform when a scheduled report has been generated and is available for review.
Group 7 — Role-Based Access ControlFR-26 to FR-29
FR-26
The system shall enforce three distinct access levels: Provincial Administrator, Municipal User, and NGO User.
FR-27
The system shall restrict project approval and report generation to Provincial Administrator accounts only.
FR-28
The system shall restrict Municipal Users to submitting and updating projects within their designated municipality only.
FR-29
The system shall restrict NGO Users to registering, updating, and viewing their own organisation's projects and funding records.
Group 8 — User Management & AuthenticationFR-30 to FR-33
FR-30
The system shall allow new users to register by providing their full name, organisation, role type, and email address, pending approval by a Provincial Administrator.
FR-31
The system shall authenticate users via email address and password on every login attempt.
FR-32
The system shall provide a password recovery mechanism that sends a time-limited reset link to the user's registered email address.
FR-33
The system shall automatically terminate a user session after 30 minutes of inactivity and require re-authentication to continue.
04 — Non-Functional Requirements
Quality Attributes
SecurityNFR-S
NFR-S01
RBAC with at least three permission levels enforced.
NFR-S02
Passwords stored using bcrypt hashing, work factor ≥ 10.
NFR-S03
All transmissions encrypted via HTTPS (TLS 1.2 or higher).
NFR-S04
Auto logout after 30 minutes of inactivity.
NFR-S05
Audit log of all create, update, and delete actions on project records.
PerformanceNFR-P
NFR-P01
Dashboard loads within 3 seconds under normal load of up to 50 concurrent users.
NFR-P02
Search returns within 2 seconds for datasets of up to 10 000 records.
NFR-P03
Automated report generation completes within 30 seconds for up to 500 records.
UsabilityNFR-U
NFR-U01
Core tasks completable within 5 minutes without prior training.
NFR-U02
All form fields include contextual help text or tooltips.
NFR-U03
Meaningful error messages for all invalid inputs specifying the field and nature of error.
NFR-U04
UI fully functional on screen widths from 768px (tablet) to 1024px (desktop).
AvailabilityNFR-A
NFR-A01
99% uptime during government working hours (07:00–17:00, Mon–Fri).
NFR-A02
Scheduled maintenance communicated to all users at least 48 hours in advance.
NFR-A03
Automated daily database backups retained for a minimum of 30 days.
MaintainabilityNFR-M
NFR-M01
All source code follows a documented coding standard with inline comments for non-obvious logic.
NFR-M02
Three-tier architecture separating presentation, business logic, and data layers.
ComplianceNFR-C
NFR-C01
POPIA compliance for all user data collected, stored, and processed.
NFR-C02
All report outputs aligned with DFFE and NDC reporting templates where applicable.

06 — Entity Relationships
Cardinality Table
RelationshipCardinalityDescription
User → Project1 to manyOne user can manage many projects
Project → SectorMany to 1Many projects belong to one sector
Project → ImpactMetric1 to manyOne project can have many impact measurement records
Project → Report1 to manyOne project can have many reports generated
User → Report1 to manyOne user can create many reports
Sector → PolicyTarget1 to manyOne sector can have many NDC policy targets
Project → ProjectFunding1 to manyOne project can have many funding allocation records
ProjectFunding → FundingMany to 1Many allocation records link to one funding window
Funding → DonorMany to 1Many funding windows can come from one donor
Project ↔ FundingMany to manyResolved by ProjectFunding junction entity

08 — Research & Literature Review
Environmental Challenges
Air Quality
Highveld Priority Area
Formally declared an Air Quality Priority Area. Coal-fired power stations emit 82% of South Africa's SO₂ and 73% of NOₓ nationally — both exceeding WHO and SA standards. In 2022, courts ruled against the government for failing to implement its own Air Quality Management Plan.
82% of SA SO₂73% of SA NOₓ
Water Quality
Olifants River System
One of the most polluted rivers in Southern Africa. Dissolved sulphate levels at the Wolwekrans monitoring station reached up to seven times the safe threshold for human consumption. Approximately ten million people depend on the river for water.
10M people dependent7× safe sulphate limit
Agriculture & Climate
Variability & Arable Land
Measurable long-term shifts in temperature and rainfall, with declining summer precipitation threatening the agricultural sector. The province contains approximately 46% of South Africa's high-potential arable land, with coal mining expansion placing this at risk.
46% of SA arable landDeclining summer rain
Biodiversity
Ecosystem Degradation
Mining and prospecting rights cover approximately 61% of Mpumalanga's land area, placing extraordinary pressure on natural habitats including the Kruger National Park and Blyde River Canyon. Fish and crocodile deaths in Kruger have been linked to upstream copper sulphate contamination.
61% under mining rights
Waste & Land
Mine Legacy & AMD
A century of coal mining has left underground mine fires, acid mine drainage (AMD) from abandoned and active workings, and thousands of hectares of unrehabilitated open-cast pit land. AMD is described as one of the mining industry's largest environmental liabilities globally.
190km Witbank coalfield
Research Conclusion
Why EcoTrack is Needed
No other South African province faces this combination simultaneously: primary carbon emitter, biodiversity corridor, critical freshwater catchment, and food production zone — yet the environmental monitoring infrastructure remains fragmented, inconsistent, and in many areas entirely absent.
09 — Deliverables
Presentation 2 Checklist
TASK 01
Interview client. Document problem, vision, and scope.
TASK 02
List functional requirements with IDs (FR-01, FR-02…)
TASK 03
Create Level 0 DFD (context diagram)
TASK 04
Create Level 1 DFD (expand main processes)
TASK 05
Create conceptual class diagram (entities + relationships)
TASK 06
Assemble presentation. Present to client for feedback.

Presentation 2 · 4 May 2026
APB J Black 10 · 08:00–12:30
Presentation 3 — Requirement Analysis

Wireframes & UI Design

APB J Black 10
08:00 – 12:30
10–15 minute presentation
P3 — 01 · Use Cases
Core Use Cases
UC-01 Register a New Climate Project
Municipal User / NGO User / Admin
Pre-condition
User is authenticated and has a role that permits project registration (Municipal User, NGO User, or Provincial Admin).
Main Flow
1.User navigates to the Dashboard and clicks Register New Project.
2.System presents the Registering New Project form.
3.User completes all required fields: project name, sector, type (Mitigation/Adaptation), location, start date, status, and GHG estimate.
4.User maps the project to at least one NDC target.
5.User clicks Submit for Approval.
6.System validates all fields. If valid, creates the project record with status Pending and sends an approval notification to the Provincial Admin.
7.System displays a success confirmation message and returns the user to the Project Page.
Alternate Flow
A.If validation fails: system highlights the invalid fields with inline error messages (NFR-U03) and the user remains on the form to correct.
B.User clicks Save as Draft: system saves the record with status Draft and returns to Project Page without triggering an approval notification.
C.Admin submits: record is approved immediately without the Pending stage.
Post-condition
A new project record exists in the system with status Pending (or Planned for Admin submissions). The project appears in the Project Page list and is visible on the Dashboard sector count.
UC-02 Log Environmental Impact Metrics
NGO User / Municipal User / Admin
Pre-condition
User is authenticated. An active project record exists that belongs to the user's organisation scope. The project's status is Active.
Main Flow
1.User navigates to the Project Overview for the relevant project.
2.User clicks Add Impact Metric, opening the Project Impact Verification screen.
3.User selects the Metric Type from the dropdown (e.g. GHG Reduction in CO₂e, Hectares Restored, Water Quality Index).
4.User enters the Measured Value with unit, the Measurement Date, and methodology notes.
5.Optionally, user attaches a photo or document as supporting evidence.
6.User clicks Submit Metric.
7.System saves the ImpactMetric record, updates the cumulative Dashboard total (FR-09), and returns the user to Project Overview with the new metric visible in the Impact tab.
Alternate Flow
A.Required fields left empty: system highlights missing fields and user must complete them before submitting.
Post-condition
A new ImpactMetric record is linked to the project. The Dashboard cumulative emissions total is updated. The metric appears in the project's impact history timeline. An entry is added to the Project Activity Log.
UC-03 Generate a Quarterly Environmental Report
Provincial Admin / Policy Analyst
Pre-condition
User is authenticated as a Provincial Administrator or Policy Analyst. At least one project exists with status Active for the selected quarter.
Main Flow
1.User navigates to the Report Page.
2.User selects Report Type: Quarterly Environmental Report.
3.User selects the quarter and year (e.g. Q1 2026).
4.Optionally applies Sector and Municipality filters.
5.User clicks Generate Report.
6.System compiles emissions data, NDC alignment status, and funding expenditure for the selected period (FR-23) and renders a preview on screen.
7.User reviews the preview and clicks Download PDF (FR-24).
8.System generates the PDF aligned to DFFE and NDC templates (NFR-C02) and saves it to the report archive. A platform notification is sent to designated users (FR-25).
Alternate Flow
A.No data for the selected period: system displays an empty state message rather than generating a blank report.
B.Report generation exceeds 30 seconds: system displays a progress indicator and delivers the report asynchronously with a notification when ready (NFR-P03).
Post-condition
A PDF report is generated, saved to the archive, and available for download. Designated users receive an in-platform notification that the report is ready. The report appears in the Previously Generated Reports list with date and type metadata.
UC-04 Link a Funding Source to a Project
Provincial Admin / NGO User
Pre-condition
User is authenticated. An active project record exists in scope. At least one Funding Window has been created by the Admin.
Main Flow
1.User navigates to Project Overview → Finance tab → Project Finance screen.
2.User clicks Link Funding Source, opening a modal.
3.User selects an existing Funding Window from a dropdown (showing donor name and available balance).
4.User enters the Allocation Amount in ZAR and the Allocation Date.
5.User confirms. System creates a ProjectFunding junction record (FR-19) and updates the Funding Window's remaining balance.
6.Project Finance screen refreshes with the new allocation visible.
Alternate Flow
A.Allocation amount exceeds the Funding Window's remaining balance: system displays a warning and prevents the over-allocation.
Post-condition
A ProjectFunding record links the project to the selected funding window. The Finance Page portfolio totals are updated. The Activity Log records the funding linkage event.
UC-05 Review Donor Portfolio and Download Report
Donor
Pre-condition
User is authenticated as a Donor. At least one project is linked to a funding window associated with this donor.
Main Flow
1.Donor logs in and lands on the Donor Dashboard.
2.Donor reviews aggregate stats: total committed, total spent, total remaining, and GHG attributed.
3.Donor clicks a project in the funded list to view its Project Overview (read-only).
4.Donor views the Finance tab to see their specific allocation and expenditure for that project.
5.Donor returns to Donor Dashboard and clicks Download Portfolio Report.
6.System generates a scoped PDF showing only the donor's funded projects, spend, and impact — aligned to NDC reporting templates.
Alternate Flow
A.Donor navigates to a project not in their portfolio: system displays a permission denied message and prevents access.
Post-condition
Donor has reviewed their portfolio and downloaded a PDF report scoped to their funding window. No changes were made to any system records (Donor is read-only).
P3 — 02 · UI Flow Diagram
Screen Inventory
2.1 — Shared / Authentication Screens
ALL ROLES
Login Page
Purpose
Entry point for all users. Authenticates returning users and routes them to the correct role-based landing screen based on their assigned role.
Key Elements
Email & password fields · Show/hide toggle · Remember me checkbox · Login button · Forgot Password link · Sign Up link · Inline error messaging · Post-login routing by role (Admin → Admin Dashboard, NGO → NGO Dashboard, Donor → Donor Dashboard)
ALL ROLES
Sign Up Page
Purpose
Allows new users to request system access. Captures identity and organisational data. Submissions are pending until a Provincial Administrator approves the account (FR-30).
Key Elements
Full name · Email · Password + confirm (strength indicator) · Organisation name · Role type dropdown · Municipality selector (conditional) · T&C checkbox · Submit button · Pending approval confirmation message
2.2 — Core Application Screens
ADMINNGO
Dashboard
Purpose
Central hub providing a real-time overview of all climate project activity scoped to the logged-in user. Province-wide for Admins; municipality-scoped for Municipal Users; organisation-scoped for NGO Users.
Key Elements
Active projects count · Cumulative CO₂e reduced · Funding allocated (ZAR) · Overdue projects alert (FR-12) · Sector distribution chart (FR-11) · NDC alignment progress (FR-15) · Register New Project & Generate Report quick actions
ADMINNGO
Project Page
Purpose
Master list view of all climate projects in scope. Primary browsing and discovery interface. Satisfies FR-05 — search and filter by sector, municipality, type, status, and date range.
Key Elements
Keyword search · Filter panel (Sector, Municipality, Type, Status, Date Range) · Sortable project table with sector badge, status, last updated · Pagination · Register New Project button (role-gated) · Export CSV
ADMINNGO
Registering New Project
Purpose
Data-capture form for logging a new climate project into the system. Designed to be completable within 5 minutes without prior training (NFR-U01). Satisfies FR-01, FR-02, FR-03, FR-06.
Key Elements
Project name · Sector multi-select (min 1, FR-02) · Type: Mitigation/Adaptation + notes (FR-03) · Location/municipality · Start & end dates · Status dropdown · GHG estimate (FR-06) · Vulnerability indicator (FR-08) · NDC target mapping (FR-14) · Submit / Save Draft / Cancel · Contextual tooltips on all fields (NFR-U02)
ADMINNGODONOR
Project Overview
Purpose
Read-only detail view for a single project. Aggregates all project data across modules — registration details, status, impact metrics, funding, NDC alignment, and activity history. Single source of truth for any climate project.
Key Elements
Project header (name, sector badges, status, municipality) · Summary tab · Impact tab · Finance tab → Project Finance · NDC Alignment tab · Activity Log tab → Project Activity Log · Edit Details button (role-restricted) · Add Impact Metric button
ADMINNGO
Editing Project Details Page
Purpose
Editable version of the Project Overview form. Allows authorised users to update existing project records. All edits are logged in the Activity Log (NFR-S05). Municipal Users limited to their municipality (FR-28); NGOs to their own records (FR-29).
Key Elements
All registration fields pre-populated and editable · Status dropdown (Planned / Active / Complete) · Save Changes button · Cancel · Every save auto-creates an Activity Log entry · Edit button hidden for users without permission on that record
ADMINNGO
Project Activity Log
Purpose
Complete chronological audit trail of all changes to a specific project record. Satisfies NFR-S05. Essential for governance, accountability, and dispute resolution.
Key Elements
Reverse-chronological entries · Each entry: action type (Created / Updated / Status Changed / Funding Linked / Impact Logged), field affected, previous & new value, user, timestamp · Filter by action type & date range · Export as PDF/CSV
ADMINNGO
Project Impact Verification
Purpose
Captures measured environmental outcomes against a project after initial registration — converting estimates into actuals. Data feeds directly into Dashboard summaries and monthly/quarterly reports.
Key Elements
Project reference header (read-only) · Metric Type dropdown (GHG Reduction, Water Quality Index, Biodiversity Score, Hectares Restored, etc.) · Measured Value + unit · Measurement Date · Methodology notes · Photo/document attachment · Impact history timeline with trend chart
ADMINDONOR
Finance Page
Purpose
Climate Finance Module master overview. Consolidated view of all funding windows, allocations, and expenditure. Satisfies FR-18 to FR-21. Donors see only their own funding windows — data confidentiality enforced at access layer.
Key Elements
Total Funds Allocated / Spent / Remaining · Funding Windows table (donor name, amount, deadline, status) · Allocation by Project bar chart · Add Funding Window button (Admin only) · Export Funding Summary Report (PDF, FR-21) · Filter by period and donor type
ADMINNGODONOR
Project Finance
Purpose
Project-level financial detail view. Shows funding sources linked to a single project, allocation amounts, actual spend, and remaining balance. Satisfies FR-19 and FR-20. Accessible via the Finance tab on Project Overview.
Key Elements
Project reference header · Funding Sources table (donor, allocated ZAR, spent ZAR, remaining ZAR) · Overall spend progress bar · Link Funding Source button · Log Expenditure button · Expenditure history timeline
DONOR
Donor Dashboard
Purpose
Specialised dashboard for funding organisations. Emphasises financial accountability over project counts. Landing screen after login for the Donor role. All data automatically scoped to the Donor's funding windows.
Key Elements
Total Committed / Spent / Remaining (ZAR) · GHG attributed to donor-funded projects · Sector impact distribution · Projects Funded list (read-only) · Upcoming deadline alerts · Download Portfolio Report (PDF)
ADMINDONOR
Report Page
Purpose
Automated Reporting Engine interface. Allows users to view archived reports, trigger new generation, and configure parameters. Satisfies FR-22 to FR-25. Reports aligned to DFFE and NDC templates (NFR-C02). PDF export (FR-24).
Key Elements
Report Type selector (Monthly Progress, Quarterly Environmental, Annual, Paris Agreement Alignment, Funding Summary) · Period selector · Sector & Municipality filters · Generate Report button · Report archive with download links · Notification badge (FR-25) · On-screen preview panel
P3 — 03 · Flow Diagrams
UI Flow Diagrams
Provincial Administrator Admin UI Flow Diagram
Click to expand full screen
Overview
Shows the complete navigation paths available to the Provincial Admin: from Login → Dashboard, through Project Management (Project Page → Project Overview → Edit/Impact/Finance/Log sub-screens), New Project Registration, and Finance & Reporting flows. The Admin is the only role that can generate quarterly environmental reports and approve projects submitted by NGOs.
Navigation Paths
Path 1 — Project Management
Dashboard → Project Page → Project Overview → [Edit Details | Impact Verification | Project Finance | Activity Log]
Path 2 — Register New Project
Dashboard → Register New Project → Form → [Submit (auto-approved) | Save Draft | Cancel]
Path 3 — Finance & Reporting
Dashboard → Finance Page → [Project Finance | Report Page] → Generate → Download PDF
Climate Finance Bodies & Funding Organisations Donor UI Flow Diagram
Click to expand full screen
Overview
Narrowest flow of the three — Donors are read-only. The diagram shows the Donor landing on the Donor Dashboard and navigating to view funded projects (Project Overview), check financials (Project Finance), and download scoped reports. All write controls are hidden. Data is automatically filtered to the Donor's own funding windows.
Navigation Paths
Path 1 — Portfolio Review
Login → Donor Dashboard → Project Overview (read-only) → Project Finance → Donor Dashboard
Path 2 — Finance Overview
Donor Dashboard → Finance Page (scoped) → [Project Finance] → Donor Dashboard
Path 3 — Reporting
Donor Dashboard → Report Page (pre-filtered) → Generate → Download PDF
NGO Field Coordinators & Programme Managers NGO UI Flow Diagram
Click to expand full screen
Overview
Most operationally active flow. NGO users are the primary submitters of project data and impact metrics. The diagram shows project registration (with an approval gate before Planned status), impact logging, status updates, and funding record viewing — all scoped to the NGO's own organisation.
Navigation Paths
Path 1 — Register Project

SLIDE 02Problem StatementAsa Salima

"Imagine you are a provincial government officer. Your job is to report Mpumalanga's progress to national government. You have twelve different departments feeding you data. Each one uses a different format. Half of them are three months out of date. And your deadline is tomorrow."

12
Departments
Each siloed, using different formats, with no unified view of climate activity.
0
Shared Platform
No single system tracks climate projects across Mpumalanga Province.
83%
SA Coal Output
Mpumalanga powers South Africa — yet cannot measure its own climate response.
The Core Problem

Provincial government, municipal managers, and NGO coordinators are all trying to address climate change — but doing it in complete isolation. Duplicated effort. Wasted funding. Zero shared accountability.

Projects get funded twice. Targets get reported inaccurately. Communities that should be benefiting from climate programmes don't even know the programmes exist.

The Paris Agreement Gap

South Africa has committed to Nationally Determined Contributions under the Paris Agreement. Mpumalanga — the province with the highest emissions in the country — has no centralised system to track, measure, or report its contribution to those commitments.

"The Paris Agreement sets a target. Nobody in Mpumalanga is watching whether we're hitting it."
SLIDE 03Solution OverviewAsa Salima
What We Built

EcoTrack Mpumalanga — a web-based Provincial Climate Change Information System. One platform. One source of truth. For every stakeholder in the province.

✓  Role-based access — 3 distinct user experiences
✓  Real-time data — no emails, no spreadsheets
✓  Paris Agreement aligned — every data field
✓  Province-wide visibility across all 3 districts
3 User Experiences, 1 System
Provincial Government Officer
All active climate projects — geographically mapped, tracked against Paris Agreement targets, with reporting deadlines built in.
Municipal Manager
Their projects — what's on track, what's at risk — without calling anyone or sending a single email.
NGO Coordinator
Project updates submitted in under 2 minutes — directly into the system, instantly visible to all authorised stakeholders.
7 Core Modules
01
Project Registry
02
Emissions & Vulnerability Tracker
03

04

05

SLIDE 05Key Design DecisionsKamogelo Selepe

Three decisions defined this product — each rooted in our understanding of who actually uses it and how they work.

DECISION 01
Simplicity over features
We could have built twenty modules. We built seven that actually work. Our primary users are government officials — not developers. Every feature in EcoTrack exists because a stakeholder needed it. Nothing was added to look impressive. A system nobody uses solves nothing.
DECISION 02
Designed for low digital literacy
Every action in EcoTrack requires a maximum of four clicks. Our least tech-comfortable test user completed core tasks — logging a project and viewing the dashboard — without any guidance and within five minutes. The interface uses plain language, contextual tooltips on every form field, and error messages that tell users exactly what went wrong and how to fix it.
DECISION 03
Every data field aligned to the Paris Agreement reporting framework
This was not an accident. The data collected in EcoTrack feeds directly into national and international climate reporting — without any translation layer. Officers log it once. It reports correctly. This is what makes EcoTrack genuinely useful to government, not just a digital filing cabinet.
SLIDE 06User Feedback & TestingMunashe Mutamangira

We tested with real users from our target stakeholder groups and changed the product based on what they told us. The prototype you see today is version two.

Finding 01
Navigation
Users found the initial navigation confusing — too many menu levels. Information was buried and core functions were difficult to find.
→ Flattened navigation from 3 levels to 2. Task completion time improved by 40%.
Finding 02
Dashboard
Stakeholders wanted to see project status at a glance — not buried in detail pages. Decision-makers needed the most critical information on the first screen.
→ Redesigned dashboard to surface status indicators. Clicks to get a province overview reduced from 9 to 3.
Finding 03
Reporting
Government officers needed export capability for their existing reporting workflows. PDF was the required format for national department submissions.
→ Added one-click PDF export to every report view.
"The product you see today is version two — shaped by the people who will actually use it."
SLIDE 07Technical ArchitectureMunashe Mutamangira
Architecture Overview

EcoTrack is built on a 7-module architecture sharing a single relational database — data entered in one module is instantly available across all others. No silos. No duplication.

Our ERD has 7 core entities: User, Project, Organisation, Report, GHGRecord, Document, and Notification — each mapping directly to a real-world process in provincial climate governance.

✓  Three-tier architecture (Presentation / Logic / Data)
✓  RBAC implemented at database level
✓  HTTPS TLS 1.2+ encryption in transit
✓  bcrypt password hashing (work factor ≥10)
✓  POPIA compliant data model
✓  Full audit log on all create/update/delete actions
Security Architecture

Role-based access control is implemented at the database level — not just the UI. Even if someone manipulates a URL, they cannot access data their role does not permit. Security was an architectural decision from day one, not an afterthought.

PERFORMANCE TARGETS
Dashboard load: < 3 seconds (50 concurrent users)
Search results: < 2 seconds (10,000 records)
Report generation: < 30 seconds (500 projects)
System uptime: 99% during government working hours
SLIDE 08Limitations & ChallengesThando Mashele

No system is perfect at version two. We know exactly where ours falls short — and we know exactly what we are going to do about it.

Offline Capability
NEXT SPRINT
Mpumalanga's rural areas have inconsistent internet connectivity. EcoTrack currently requires an active connection. Our next sprint adds a Progressive Web App layer — users can log data offline and sync when connectivity returns. This also bypasses government IT procurement timelines, since a PWA runs in any browser without app store approval.
Integration with Existing Government Systems
NEXT PHASE
EcoTrack currently operates as a standalone platform. Several departments already have legacy systems in use. We have mapped the API integration points — that work begins in the next phase. The three-tier architecture means integration touches the data layer without requiring changes to the interface.
Scale Testing
SCOPED & COSTED
We tested with a small user group. A province-wide rollout requires load testing at significantly higher volume across all three districts. That infrastructure work is scoped and costed — we know exactly what it requires and what it will take to get there.
SLIDE 09Next Steps & ViabilityThando Mashele
0–3 Months
Foundation
Progressive Web App offline capability
API integration framework for legacy systems
Formal pilot with one provincial department
Load testing at province-wide volume
3–6 Months
Expansion
Province-wide pilot — all 3 stakeholder groups
Full support and training documentation
DFFE reporting template integration
Version 3 feedback-driven release
6–12 Months
Scale
Model adopted by other provinces in SA
Private sector licence model activated
National CCIS integration
Self-sustaining revenue stream established
Financial Viability

Provincial government is the client. South Africa spends billions annually on climate adaptation. EcoTrack reduces reporting costs, eliminates duplicated project funding, and improves accountability across the province.

Cloud hosting: R8,000–R15,000 / month
vs. manual reporting across 12 departments
= measurable, significant return on investment
Revenue Model

Mining houses, energy companies, and corporate ESG departments in Mpumalanga are all under pressure to report climate impact. EcoTrack supports a tiered access model — corporates pay a licence to plug their projects into the same provincial system.

One platform. Two revenue streams.
Government accountability + Private sector compliance = a self-sustaining product.
SLIDE 10Questions & DiscussionSanele Belinda Dlela
"Mpumalanga is the energy heartbeat of South Africa. And for too long, the work being done to protect that province from climate change has been invisible."
"EcoTrack Mpumalanga makes that work visible."
"Nine students. A system that is genuinely ready to be used."
"We are Team Artemi9. And this is EcoTrack Mpumalanga."
